coredata.dc:title: The impact of immerssion time and thickness of TiO2 photoanode on power conversion efficiency of dye-sensitised solar cells using graphene quantum dots as photosensitiser
coredata.dc:creator: Zamiri, Golnoush
Bagheri, Samira
Babadi, Arman Amani
Moosavi, Seyedehmaryam
Naghavi, M.S.
coredata.dc:description: Scientists propose developing stable, efficient, cost-effective, and colorful ruthenium-free dyes to improve the performance of dye-sensitised solar cells (DSSCs), facilitating their commercialization and overcoming the limitation of ruthenium-based dyes. Therefore, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a photosensitiser, having unique and fascinating properties, have attracted the interest of researchers for them to be used in the fabrication of green DSSC based on titanium oxide nanoparticles (TiO2-NPs) as a photoanode. GQDs bear both the individual and attractive properties of the graphene nature as well as the size-resulted quantum effects. In addition to the unique electrical and optical properties of GQDs, their bandgap is lesser than 2.0 eV making them interesting material in the field of DSSCs. In this article, we fabricate a TiO2-NPs based DSSCs using GQDs green photosensitiser and investigate the impact of immersion time and photoanode layer thickness on the performance and power conversion efficiency (PCE) of fabricated DSSC. The optimized immersion time of TiO2 in GQDs green photosensitiser is analyzed to be 21 h and 40 μm thickness of the photoanode layer. The I–V result test indicates open circuit output potential difference (Voc) of 0.74 V, and short circuit current (ISC) of 6.62 mA with PCE of 2.76% and a fill factor (FF) of 56.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dye-sensitised solar cells
TiO2 photoanode
Graphene quantum dots
Green photosensitiser

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of TiO2 nanoparticles using hydrothermal technique
Preparation of GQD by hydrothermal method
Impact of immersion time of TiO2-PA on the PCE of DSSCs
Impact of thickness of TiO2-PA on PCE of DSSCs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is financially supported by the University Malaya Research Grant (PG101-2014B) and (RP038B-15HTM).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) TEM image of prepared TiO2-NPs and b) HRTEM image of GQDs.
Current-voltage (I–V) plot of TiO2-PA based DSSC with different duration of immersion time in GQDs photosensitiser.
FESEM microgram of TiO2-PA with a variety of thicknesses.
a) Current-voltage (I–V) and b) IPCE plots of DSSCs using GQDs photosensitiser with five different thickness of TiO2-PA.
a) TiO2-PA, GQDs, and N-719 Vacuum energy level and b) PL spectra of TiO2-PA, TiO2-PA immerse in GQDs and TiO2-PA immersed in N-719 photosensitisers.
Current-voltage (I–V) curves of DSSCs devices based on TiO2 nanoparticles using N-719 as a photosensitiser and GQDs as a photosensitiser.
Nyquist plot of TiO2-PA based DSSC using N-719 photosensitiser and GQD photosensitiser.
IPCE plot of TiO2-PA based DSSC using GQD photosensitiser.
The role of immersion time in photovoltaic properties of DCCS devices based on TiO2-NPs.
Photovoltaic properties fabricated DCCSs using GQDs photosensitiser with different TiO2-PA thicknesses.
Compression of recent DSSC employing the GQD photosensitiser with variety of photoanodes.

head.ce:title: The impact of immerssion time and thickness of TiO2 photoanode on power conversion efficiency of dye-sensitised solar cells using graphene quantum dots as photosensitiser

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Mechanical Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Universiti Malaya, 50603, Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Scientists propose developing stable, efficient, cost-effective, and colorful ruthenium-free dyes to improve the performance of dye-sensitised solar cells (DSSCs), facilitating their commercialization and overcoming the limitation of ruthenium-based dyes. Therefore, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a photosensitiser, having unique and fascinating properties, have attracted the interest of researchers for them to be used in the fabrication of green DSSC based on titanium oxide nanoparticles (TiO2-NPs) as a photoanode. GQDs bear both the individual and attractive properties of the graphene nature as well as the size-resulted quantum effects. In addition to the unique electrical and optical properties of GQDs, their bandgap is lesser than 2.0 eV making them interesting material in the field of DSSCs. In this article, we fabricate a TiO2-NPs based DSSCs using GQDs green photosensitiser and investigate the impact of immersion time and photoanode layer thickness on the performance and power conversion efficiency (PCE) of fabricated DSSC. The optimized immersion time of TiO2 in GQDs green photosensitiser is analyzed to be 21 h and 40 μm thickness of the photoanode layer. The I–V result test indicates open circuit output potential difference (Voc) of 0.74 V, and short circuit current (ISC) of 6.62 mA with PCE of 2.76% and a fill factor (FF) of 56.

ce:section.ce:para: In 1991, DSSC was invented with many promising potentials having the capability to replace the commonly used silicon-based solar cells. Since then, it has attracted worldwide attention due to its environmentally friendly properties and its efficient ability of using indoor light sources while keeping the manufacturing process easy and inexpensive. Although the theoretical photoelectric conversion efficiency (η) of DSSC has been reported to increase up to 33%, the experimental units fabricated in the laboratory using the sequential deposition technique have a maximum efficiency of 13% [1]. There are numerous requirements for an efficient photo-sensitizer for DSSCs such as, (1) strong absorption and broad spectral range to maximize light harvesting, (2) higher than the edge of the conduction band of transition metal oxides (TMOs) of the dye's LUMO level to facilitate electron injection, (3) containment of a carboxylate or phosphonate group to anchor on the surface of a semiconductor oxide, (4) the oxidized dye holding a more positive potential than the redox electrochemical potential in the electrolyte to accept electron donation from an electrolyte or a conductive hole material and (5) long term stability and chemical stability under illumination [2]. Normally, photo-anode in DSSCs supports photosensitiser loading and transport photo-excited electrons from the sensitizer to the external circuit. TiO2 nanostructures attracted researchers' attention due to their large surface area, which helps dye absorption, electrolyte penetration, light scattering, and multi reflection via its visible light harvesting capability. It is required to determine the perfect thickness of TiO2 photo-anode (TiO2-PA) and immersion time in the proposed dye to improve the performance of DSSCs.

Ruthenium-based complexes are the most utilized sensitizer in DSSCs [3]. However, despite their advantages, ruthenium is not an abundant earth material. In order to overcome this lack in the resources, quantum dots are introduced as an alternative to ruthenium based dyes in DSSCs because of their broad tunable absorption spectrum (from ultraviolet to near-infrared) [4,5]. Among different kinds of quantum dots, carbon-based quantum dots such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been recently used in DSSCs application due to their high PL quantum yield, chemical stability, low toxicity, and very high optical absorptivity [6,7]. Zero-dimensional (0D) GQDs are described as a single layer or few layers of graphene possessing with the size of 20 nm or less. The bandgap of GQDs is below 2.0 eV [8] owing it to their quantum size and edge effects. Replacing ruthenium-based dye with GQDs as a green photo-sensitizer is seen as a new approach for higher efficiency DSSCs due to bandgap varies with the size of GQDs [9,10]. According to Li, Hu [11], reducing the size of GQD, not only can efficiently adjust the bandgap but it also can facilitate its wide variety of applications. Previous studies have reported the modification of photoelectrodes through using the composite of GQD/TiO2 might improve the efficiency of DSSCs. Xiaoli Fang and his co-workers prepared GQDs assisting dye-Sensitised TiO2-PA. They investigated the influences of the GQDs on the properties of TiO2 photo-anode and the DSSCs [12]. Kim, Lee [13] reported that the performance of DSSCs was improved by utilizing GQD (approximately 5.0–10.0 nm) TiO2-PA layers. In our previous research, we successfully prepared GQDs through the hydrothermal procedure and investigate the performance of DSSCs using ZnO photo-anode (ZnO-PA) by substituting N-719 with GQDs as a photo-sensitizer. Since the efficiency of DSSC using ZnO-PA immersed in GQDs is similar to devices using N-719, the GQDs can be an alternative to ruthenium-based dyes [14]. The photo-anode plays an important role to increase the PCE of DSSCs. To improve the efficiency of DSSCs using GQDs as photosensitiser, it is necessary to analyze the effect of immersion time and the thickness of photoanode.

In this research, we successfully fabricated DSSCs based on TiO2-NPs as a photoelectrode and GQDs as a photosensitiser. In addition, to enhance and improve the PCE of fabricated DSSC, the impact of immersion time in photosensitiser along with the thickness of TiO2 based photoanode were optimized. Consequently, the photovoltaic performance of fabricated DSSC was studied by current-voltage (I–V) and incident photon to current conversion efficiency (IPCE), indicating the immersion time of 21 h with the thickness of 40 μm has the highest power conversion efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of TiO2 nanoparticles using hydrothermal technique
ce:section.ce:para: In order to prepare TiO2-NPs using hydrothermal technique, titanium isopropoxide was added in the mixture of ethanol and distilled water solution at pH 0.7 with nitric acid. The mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined sealed stainless steel autoclaves and heated in the oven at 240 °C for 4 h. The white perticipate was washed with ethanol and distilled water several times and then dried in the oven at 75 °C for 6 h [15].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GQD by hydrothermal method
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrothermal method, described in our previous study [14], was employed in order to synthesize GQD. The employed GO in this study is synthesized from graphite using the modified Hummers’ method [16]. The GO gel was mixed with 40 mL nitric acid (2.6 M) for 24 h under reflux at 70 °C. The next day, the gel was allowed to naturally cool down to room temperature. Then the solution was ultra-sonicated for 60 min and ﬁltered through two 0.22 mm microporous membrane, followed by autoclaving the mixture in a 40 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel vessel at 200 °C for 24 h.

The crystalline structures of TiO2-NPs was analyzed by an X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (Bruker-D8) fitted with Cu-Kα X-ray radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å). The morphological study of TiO2-NPs was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The specific surface area of TiO2-NP were determined by Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET analysis).

Each component of DSSC can influence the power conversion efficiency (PCE) parameters. Variables which can alter the photo-voltages and short-circuit photocurrents include how far is the PA located from the counter-electrode, and the quality of materials and electrical contacts points on the surface of the electrode. So, physical and chemical manufacturing should be standardized to improve the efficiency of the fabricated devices.

Square pieces of 2 × 2 cm were cut out of Fluorine-doped Tin oxide glass (FTO- glass) and soaked for 15 min inside the detergent solution. After that, they were ultrasonicated (5 min) in the solution of acetone, ethanol, and DW sequentially. The TiO2 paste was developed by addition of 1 g of P25 to the mixture of pure ethanol (5 mL), Triton-X100 (2 mL), and DW (0.5 mL) followed by magnetic stirring for 20 min at ambient temperature. Then, following the doctor-blade method, the obtained TiO2 pastes were placed on the surface of a pre-cleaned FTO-glass. Final steps include preparing the counter electrode, dropping a platinum-ethanol solution drop on the surface of the FTO-glass and its subsequent frottage. The DSSC will be sandwiched between the TiO2/FTO-glass as photoanode and the counter electrode (cathode). An ionic liquid electrolyte Z-100 was injected between two electrodes.
Solar simulator with AM 1.5 is employed to characterize the photovoltaic performances of DSSCs. Furthermore, photovoltaic SR_EQE_IQE_Mapping is used to measure external quantum efficiency (EQE).
In order to assess the efficiency of the fabricated DSSC and compare it with previous studies, the following photovoltaic properties should be calculated through the related equation;

The fill factor (FF) is the maximum output power of fabricated DSSC calculated through equation (1) [17]:
(1)
F F =
V
m a x
×
I
m a x
V
O C
×
I
S C
where, VOC and ISC are open circuit voltage and short circuit current, respectively. Vmax and Imax are the voltage and current at maximum power point. Power conversion efficiency (PCE) is defined as the ratio of maximum electrical energy output to the energy input from the sun. The mathematical equation is:
(2)
η =
V
O C
×
I
S C
× F F
P
i n
where, Pin is the power input from the sunlight. Incident Photon to Charge Carrier Efficiency (IPCE), also known as an External Quantum Efficiency (EQE), is the efficiency of a solar cell in producing photo-generated charge at a given frequency according to equation (3) [18]:
(3)
I P C E
( λ )
= 1240 ×
I
S C
λ × φ
where, λ is the wavelength (nm) and Φ is the incident radiative light flux (W/m2).

The crystaline structure of TiO2-NPs was investigated by XRD analysis and is indicated in Fig. 1 . The XRD pattern of the prepared sample can be perfectly indexed to the anatase phase of TiO2-NPs according to the TiO2 anatase (JCPDS Card no. 21–1272) and no diffraction peaks from any other impurities were detected.

To study the morphology of TiO2-NPs synthesized by hydrothermal technique TEM was utilized (Fig. 2 (a)). The average particle size of the prepared sample is around 50 nm. From TEM image, it can be seen that the nanoparticles show heterogeneity in shape and size. HRTEM image of as-prepared GQDs is indicated in Fig. 2 (b). The average diameter of GQDs was around 10 nm.

BET analysis was used to determine the specific surface area of TiO2-NPs. Fig. 3 indicates a type IV isotherm typical for mesoporous materials with a hysteresis loop. This hysteresis is an intermediate between typical H1 and H2 type hysteresis loop in the relative pressure range (p/p 0) 0.4–1.0 suggesting large uniform mesopores with a cage-like pore structure connected by windows with a small size. The specific surface area is determined from the isotherms to be 52.60 m2/g based on the BET model [19].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of immersion time of TiO2-PA on the PCE of DSSCs
ce:section.ce:para: TiO2 paste was coated on the FTO glasses and dipped in GQDs photosensitiser for 7, 14, 21, and 28 h in order to investigate the optimum immersion time to enhance the performance and power outcomes of DSSCs. Fig. 4 shows the I–V plot of TiO2-PA based DSSC with different durations of immersion time in GQDs photosensitiser. The performance and power conversion efficiency significantly increased by 250% (from 0.41 to 1.09%) by extending the immersion time from 7 to 21 h. According to this figure, there is a direct correlation between the performance efficiency and the duration of immersion time up to 21 h. The 21 h is a turning point beyond which the performance efficiency decreases.

According to Table 1 , the photovoltaic properties of DSSC were reduced by immersing the TiO2-PA in GQDs photosensitiser for 28 h and the PCE and ISC values were decreased by 40% and 35%, respectively. It is shown that the highest values for ISC (2.57 mA) and PCE (1.097%) were observed when the immersion in GQDs had the duration of 21 h. This could be due to higher adsorption of more photosensitiser molecules through increasing the immersion time leading to superior light-harvesting efficiency. However, as the immersion time took longer than 21 h, the ISC and PCE dropped significantly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of thickness of TiO2-PA on PCE of DSSCs
ce:section.ce:para: According to recent studies, fabricated DSSCs performance might be altered by varying the thickness of photo-anode [20]. Hence, finding the optimum thickness is a critical step to enhance the performance and optimize the power output of fabricated DSSC. Through increasing the thickness, more photosensitiser molecules could be presented on the semiconductor film enhancing the sunlight harvesting efficiency and causing a higher current flow. However, the thicker photo-anode would force the photogenerated electrons to travel a longer distance to reach the working electrode, thus, increasing the resistance and subsequently reducing the electron recombination rate. Therefore, the current drops when the thickness is increased beyond the optimal thickness [21].

In order to study the impact of TiO2-PA thickness on DSSC characteristics, several FTO glasses were coated with various numbers of TiO2-NPs layers to fabricate the photoanodes possessing different thicknesses. The FESEM images, as shown in Fig. 5 , clearly confirm that each layer of TiO2-NPs has a mean thickness value of almost 10 μm.

With the purpose of investigating the impact of TiO2-PA thickness on the performance of DSSCs using GQD photosensitiser, 5 individual FTO glasses were coated with different numbers of TiO2-NPs layers [20]. The fabricated photoanodes were soaked in a GQDs solution for 21 h. Consequently, Fig. 6 (a) indicates the obtained I–V characteristic plot for DSSCs with various thicknesses of TiO2-NPs (total TiO2-NPs thickness of ∼10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 μm). Fig. 6 (a) claims that by increasing the thickness of TiO2-NPs layers on top of the FTO glass, the efficiency of DCCS would increase by 250% (from 1.097 to 2.76%).

The sensitivity of a DSSC is a function of the wavelength of the incident light. IPCE measures the ratio of the number of electrons generated by the solar cell to the number of incident photons on the active surface under monochromatic light irradiation. Fig. 6 (b) presents the IPCE of fabricated DSSCs with various thicknesses of TiO2-NPs. The maximum IPCE value (28%) at 510 nm is correlated to TiO2-PA with a thickness of 40 μm. The IPCE plot confirms that increasing the thickness of TiO2-PA would significantly improve light-harvesting.

Furthermore, the other photovoltaic factors of DSSC would vary in response to the altering of TiO2-PA thickness listed in Table 2 . According to Table 2, the PCE and ISC were increasing from 1.097% to 2.76% and from 2.75 mA to 6.62 mA, respectively, by increasing the TiO2-PA thickness from 10 to 40 μm. These findings confirmed the study conducted by Mohammadpour, Moradi [22] which claimed that an increase in the thickness of the TiO2 layer would improve the DSSC performance. This could be attributed to the fact that the thicker TiO2-PA can adsorb more photosensitiser molecules eventually resulting in an enhanced light-harvesting efficiency [23]. As expected, TiO2 film with the lowest thickness of 10 μm showed to be the less efficient (PCE = 1.097%) DSSC in this study. However, increasing the TiO2-PA thickness to 50 μm caused a decrease in ISC and PCE compared to the DSSC with a thickness of 40 μm TiO2-PA. It was due to the long distance that the photogenerated electron needed to pass through to reach the electrode that the resistance increased, and the performance efficiency decreased.

The vacuum energy levels of TiO2-PA, TiO2-PA immersed in GQDs, and TiO2-PA immersed in N-719 are presented in Fig. 7 (a). As indicated in this figure, the TiO2 conduction band energy level (−4.26 eV) is much closer to GQD (−3.82 eV) compare to N-719 (−3.01 eV). Since the optimum TiO2-PA thickness for GQD photosensitiser is 40 μm, this thickness is used for fabricating the DSSC for further analysis. In addition, the PL spectra of these nanomaterials are demonstrated in Fig. 7 (b). According to the PL spectra, TiO2 coated FTO glass has the emissions peak at 549 nm while immersing it in GQDs and N-719 would increase the maximum peak up to 559 nm and 569 nm, respectively. Moreover, the TiO2 coated FTO glass immersed in GQDs has the highest PL intensity in this study due to the rapid recombination of photoinduced charge carriers [24,25].

The I–V plot of TiO2-PA immersed in GQDs and the TiO2-PA immersed in N-719 is demonstrated in Fig. 8 . The TiO2-PA immersed in GQDs indicates the VOC of 0.738 V, ISC of 6.62 mA and PCE of 2.76%. Meanwhile, the TiO2-PA immersed in N-719 has the VOC of 0.790 V, ISC of 5.89 mA and PCE of 2.89%. Although the VOC of TiO2-PA immersed in GQD is lower than that of the TiO2-PA immersed in N-719, its ISC value has increased significantly leading to the insignificant difference in their PCE.

The energy level of GQD is much closer to that of TiO2 than N-719. However, the I–V test results of TiO2-PA immersed in GQD is lower than TiO2-PA immersed in N-719. This might be as a result of GQD high PL intensity leading to rapid recombination alongside the decreasing efficiency [26]. Since the PCE are almost in the same range, GQD could be used as an environmentally friendly alternative photosensitisers to N-719.
The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted in 0.01 Hz–100 kHz (dark condition) to assess the fabricated DSSC's interfacial charge transfer.

Wei, Yang [27], stated that the According to the high and low-frequency semicircles Nyquist plots attribute to the Pt counter electrode redox reaction and the electrolyte charge transfer, respectively, while the middle frequency semicircle of Nyquist plot attributes to the electron transfer at the interface of PA (TiO2/dye/electrolyte). The resistance of charge transfer from TiO2 to the electrolyte can be observed in the middle frequency area. According to Fig. 9 , fabricated DSSCs using GQDs photosensitiser shows lower Rrec compare to other devices due to the high charge recombination rate [28]. Since the N-719 photosensitiser and GQDs photosensitiser possess different interactions toward the TiO2 nanoparticles they reveal different Rct.

The IPCE plot of TiO2-PA based DSSC using GQD photosensitiser is presented in Fig. 10 . It is the function of the incident light's wavelength. The IPCE value of TiO2-PA based DSSC using GQDs photosensitiser is 28% while the TiO2-PA based DSSC using GQDs photosensitiser has the IPCE of 39%.

Table 3 summarises and compares the recent studies employing GQD photosensitiser with variety of photoanodes. According to this table, the fabricated DSSC in the current study has the best efficiency displaying an acceptable range of power output and performance.

The aim of this study was to fabricate and investigate the performance of TiO2-PA based DSSC using GQDs photosensitiser. In addition, the impact of various immersion times and thickness of TiO2-PA were assessed to optimize and enhance the performance and PCE of the fabricated DCCS. The I–V test result indicated that the immersion time of 21 h would boost the efficiency since the extension in immersion time increased photosensitiser molecules' absorption, in turn increasing the light-harvesting efficiency. Moreover, the highest PCE of 2.76% and IPCE of 28% was achieved at the TiO2-PA thickness of 40 μm using the GQD photosensitisers. Through increasing the photoanode thickness, more photosensitiser molecules could be present in the semiconductor layer to absorb sunlight resulting in the higher current generation. However, increasing thickness caused a longer path for the photogenerated electrons to reach the working electrode which increased the rate of electron recombination. Therefore, the current decreased at the post-optimal thickness. The fabricated DSSC in this study showed the best power conversion efficiency with an acceptable range of power output and performance compared to the recent studies employing GQD photosensitiser with variety of photoanodes. Although this study has provided more efficient DSSCs using GQDs as a photosensitiser, there is still a need for further study on this green alternative photosensitiser. The lower efficiency of fabricated DSSC in this study in comparison with the one in the literature employing N-719 might be due to the photoanode fabrication method (Dr. Blade's method) used to fabricate the DSSC.

Golnoush Zamiri: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Samira Bagheri: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Arman Amani Babadi: Validation, Writing – review & editing, Project administration. Seyedehmaryam Moosavi: Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Project administration. M.S. Naghavi: Investigation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the University Malaya Research Grant (PG101-2014B) and (RP038B-15HTM).
